articles.title: New York home-price movement in the latest FHFA HPI
articles.dek: Quarterly, annual, and longer-run state HPI comparisons with a clear boundary between market movement and property value.
articles.previewText: Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for New York and why an index cannot value a specific property.
articles.metaDescription: New York home-price movement in the latest FHFA HPI. Review the latest FHFA home-price movement for New York and why an index cannot value a specific property.

sections.heading: The latest statewide index reading
body: New York's latest FHFA purchase-only HPI is 419.9, accompanied by a quarterly change of -0.3%. For New York, the 419.9 index level is a benchmark for repeat-sale price movement, not a dollar-denominated typical home price. A quarter in which the index fell by -0.3% can help a borrower date the statewide market context, but it cannot identify the value or asking price of a selected property. Reading 419.9 with -0.3% also keeps the level separate from the rate of change. Alongside the 419.9 state index, local inventory, property type, condition, concessions, taxes, insurance, and financing costs still need current evidence before the trend informs a decision.

sections.heading: Quarterly and annual windows tell different stories
body: Over the latest quarter, New York's purchase-only HPI fell by -0.3%; over the latest year, it rose by 4.4%. The -0.3% figure is more sensitive to recent movement, while 4.4% compares the latest quarter with the matching quarter a year earlier. A borrower should not choose between the -0.3% and 4.4% windows based only on which looks more favorable, because the comparisons answer different timing questions. Together they show whether recent statewide movement is aligned with or different from the broader year without predicting the next quarter, a local bidding environment, or the price path of one home in New York.

sections.heading: Place the latest movement in a longer sequence
body: Across the five-year comparison, the New York HPI rose by 47.0%, compared with the latest annual movement of 4.4%. The 47.0% change places the current 419.9 index reading in a longer repeat-sale sequence, but it can span very different rate, inventory, and economic conditions. The 47.0% change should not be applied to an old purchase price to estimate a current property value. For a borrower, the contrast between 47.0% and 4.4% is most useful as a reminder that search assumptions can age at different speeds. Even when the 47.0% direction is clear, current comparable properties and a property-specific payment worksheet remain necessary.

sections.heading: Use the index to test local assumptions
body: The combination of a -0.3% quarterly change, 4.4% annual change, and 47.0% five-year change gives New York borrowers three dated views of the same statewide index. If the -0.3%, 4.4%, and 47.0% windows point in different directions, the divergence shows that timing matters; if they point together, statewide consistency still does not erase local variation. A New York search plan can use those three changes to ask whether current listings, seller concessions, and property types resemble the broad state movement. It should not use 419.9 as a sale price or use any of the percentage changes as a forecast, because FHFA HPI measures repeat-sale movement rather than the complete set of homes available to one borrower.

sections.heading: The HPI cannot value a property or set a payment
body: An HPI level of 419.9 does not establish a New York property's dollar value, and an 4.4% annual movement does not establish how much any home gained or lost. The 419.9 index excludes the borrower's down payment, loan amount, interest rate, taxes, insurance, association costs, repairs, and time horizon. FHFA can also revise published values, so the -0.3% and 47.0% comparisons should retain their release period. The 419.9 index level must remain separate from the dollar figures used in a property review. Those limits make the 419.9 state index useful for orientation but unsuitable as an appraisal, offer recommendation, equity calculation, promised appreciation claim, payment quote, or underwriting conclusion.

sections.heading: Connect the state trend to current property evidence
body: A practical New York borrower review can record -0.3%, 4.4%, and 47.0% as statewide trend markers, then compare them with current local listings and recent property-level evidence. For a purchase, keep the -0.3% state change as context while adding the selected price, inspection findings, appraisal process, taxes, insurance, cash-to-close, and loan disclosures. For a refinance or equity question, begin with a defensible property review rather than multiplying a prior value by 4.4% or 47.0%. This approach preserves the information in the 419.9 HPI while giving current local and borrower-specific facts the decision-making role that a statewide repeat-sale index cannot fill.

articles.methodology: This article uses the official FHFA purchase-only state quarterly HPI and calculates changes from matching prior-quarter, prior-year, and five-year index observations when available. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: FHFA may revise index values, statewide movement can mask local and property-type differences, and an index level is not a dollar-denominated home value. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: New York statewide labor market: latest employment evidence
articles.dek: A borrower-focused reading of the latest BLS labor-force, employment, unemployment, and rate measures for New York.
articles.previewText: See what changed in New York's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine mortgage eligibility.
articles.metaDescription: New York statewide labor market: latest employment evidence. See what changed in New York's labor market and why broad employment data cannot determine.

evidenceFacts.label: change in unemployment rate from the prior month
change in unemployment rate from a year earlier

sections.heading: The latest labor-force counts
body: The latest LAUS release reports a New York labor force of 10,094,212, with 9,631,329 people employed and 462,883 unemployed under the program's definitions. Those three counts belong together: 9,631,329 plus 462,883 explains the scale behind the published 4.6% unemployment rate. For mortgage planning, the 10,094,212 state-level labor force describes the surrounding market rather than the income or employment record of an applicant. A borrower can use the 10,094,212 labor-force baseline to understand the size of the measured market, but qualification still depends on documented income, employment continuity, debts, assets, and the rules of the loan being considered.

sections.heading: What changed from the prior month
body: New York's unemployment rate was unchanged from 4.6% in the prior month to 4.6% in the latest month, a change of 0.0%. A 0.0% move can reflect current conditions, normal sampling movement, and later revisions, so it should be read with the underlying 462,883 unemployment count and 10,094,212 labor force. The 0.0% comparison does not show that a particular employer is hiring or cutting jobs, and it cannot predict the next release. For a New York borrower, the monthly change is a prompt to date economic assumptions and verify personal employment documentation, not a reason to assume approval, denial, or a particular loan price.

sections.heading: The year-over-year comparison
body: Compared with 4.2% a year earlier, the latest New York unemployment rate of 4.6% rose by 0.4%. The year-over-year window reduces the risk of interpreting only one monthly step, but the 0.4% difference still describes a population estimate rather than an individual's job stability. Reading 4.2%, 4.6%, and 4.6% together shows whether the latest month sits within a wider movement without forecasting what follows. A borrower should keep that broad New York pattern separate from pay history, variable compensation, self-employment records, contracts, leave, job changes, and other facts a lender may need to document.

sections.heading: Why the rate needs its underlying counts
body: At 4.6%, New York's unemployment rate summarizes 462,883 unemployed people within a 10,094,212 labor force; the rate should not be discussed without those counts. The 9,631,329 employment estimate is an economic measure and does not distinguish borrowers by hours, tenure, pay structure, industry, or the documentation available for a loan file. That makes 9,631,329 useful for local context but unsuitable as evidence that one household's income is stable or sufficient. Keeping the 4.6% rate attached to 10,094,212, 9,631,329, and 462,883 prevents a small percentage movement from carrying more borrower-level meaning than the LAUS release supports.

sections.heading: Labor statistics are not an income review
body: Neither 9,631,329 employed people nor a 4.6% unemployment rate tells a lender what one New York applicant earns, whether that income is eligible, or whether it is likely to continue. The 0.0% monthly rate change also cannot substitute for pay stubs, tax returns, business records, contracts, benefit documentation, or an explanation of a recent job transition. The 4.6% rate can be relevant to timing and local economic questions while remaining outside the underwriting decision. That boundary protects borrowers from treating a favorable 0.4% year-over-year change as an approval signal or an unfavorable movement as a conclusion about their own documented capacity.

sections.heading: Turn the trend into documentation questions
body: A practical next step for a New York borrower is to note the latest 4.6% unemployment rate and the 0.4% year-over-year change, then move immediately to the records that describe the household. After noting the 4.6% rate, compare current base pay, variable earnings, self-employment history, contracts, assets, debts, and expected changes with the documentation requirements for the loan path. The 10,094,212 local labor force and 9,631,329 employment count can frame the economic conversation, but they should not alter verified borrower inputs. The 0.0% monthly movement remains a population measure even when it appears consistent with a borrower's personal experience. A licensed professional can explain which documents and time periods matter while keeping New York's 4.6% LAUS rate in its proper role as dated local economic context.

articles.methodology: This article uses BLS LAUS state monthly series for labor force, employment, unemployment, and unemployment rate, with prior-month and year-ago comparisons. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: LAUS estimates can be revised, city series are unadjusted, and local labor statistics describe a population rather than an individual applicant's income or employment. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: New York housing costs, ownership, and rent in the latest ACS
articles.dek: Statewide evidence on home value, income, owner costs, housing stock, tenure, vacancy, and rent for borrower planning.
articles.previewText: Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for New York with survey limitations and practical questions.
articles.metaDescription: New York housing costs, ownership, and rent in the latest ACS. Read statewide housing-cost, ownership, vacancy, and rent evidence for New York with survey.
keyTakeaways: prior ACS state median home value: $313,700.
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage: $2,555.

evidenceFacts.label: prior ACS state median home value
median monthly owner cost with a mortgage
change from the prior ACS home-value estimate

sections.heading: Statewide value, income, and owner costs
body: New York's 2024 ACS housing baseline reports a median owner-occupied home value of $423,800, median household income of $85,974, and median selected monthly owner costs of $2,555 for owners with a mortgage. The $423,800, $85,974, and $2,555 values are separate statewide medians, so they should not be treated as the price, income, and monthly budget of one typical household. The $2,555 measure combines recurring ownership expenses and is not a real-estate-tax figure or a current mortgage quote. For planning, $423,800, $85,974, and $2,555 identify statewide reference points and cost categories, while a real decision still requires local property, borrower, tax, insurance, and loan information.

sections.heading: Keep owner costs and rent comparable
body: The same New York release reports $1,621 in median gross rent alongside $2,555 in median selected monthly owner costs for owners with a mortgage. The two measures cover different housing populations and cost definitions, so $1,621 should not be compared with only principal and interest or used to declare a statewide break-even point. A useful comparison replaces the $1,621 and $2,555 medians with a current lease and a current property budget, including utilities, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, transaction costs, and the expected time horizon. The $85,974 statewide median can provide economic context, but dividing it by either $1,621 or $2,555 would still combine medians that do not represent the same household.

sections.heading: How occupied housing is divided by tenure
body: Across New York's measured housing stock of 8,585,241 units, the owner share of occupied housing is 54.3% and the renter share is 45.7%, making owner-occupied housing the larger statewide tenure group. The 54.3% and 45.7% shares describe how occupied units were distributed during the survey period; they do not measure current buyer demand, owner willingness to sell, or renter readiness to purchase. The 54.3% and 45.7% statewide tenure split is useful for understanding scale and checking assumptions about the housing mix. It remains too broad to select a product or characterize a particular city, county, property type, or household inside New York.

sections.heading: Vacancy is broader than homes for sale
body: New York's vacant-unit share is 10.0% within a statewide stock of 8,585,241 housing units. ACS vacancy includes categories beyond property currently offered for sale or rent, so 10.0% is not a live inventory rate and cannot measure competition for a selected home. Read beside the 54.3% owner share and 45.7% renter share, the vacancy figure helps describe how the broad stock was classified without predicting near-term supply. Borrowers should use current listings, property condition, days on market, concessions, and local professional context to answer transaction questions that 10.0% and 8,585,241 were not designed to resolve.

sections.heading: The statewide home-value estimate changed across periods
body: New York's median home-value estimate increased from $313,700 in the prior ACS period to $423,800 in the current release, a reported change of 35.1%. Because both estimates summarize multi-year survey windows, 35.1% is not a point-to-point appreciation rate for a property purchased at $313,700. The 35.1% comparison can show that an old statewide reference may need updating, but it cannot explain the value path in each local market or price segment. Margins of error, changes in the housing mix, and current property evidence should be reviewed before the move from $313,700 to $423,800 influences a purchase, refinance, or equity assumption.

sections.heading: Move from statewide context to a local worksheet
body: A borrower can carry $423,800, $2,555, $1,621, and the 10.0% vacancy share into a local comparison as dated statewide context, then replace each with evidence matched to the actual decision. For ownership, replace the $423,800 and $2,555 medians with a selected price, down payment, current loan disclosures, taxes, insurance, maintenance, association obligations, and cash reserves. For renting, replace the $1,621 median with a comparable lease, utilities, deposits, and expected changes over the same horizon. The 45.7% renter share is useful for describing the statewide tenure mix, not for predicting which local option will be available. The 54.3% tenure measure can help explain the statewide housing mix, but it cannot decide whether buying, renting, refinancing, or using equity is suitable for one New York household.

articles.methodology: This article uses 2024 ACS 5-year state estimates for home value, household income, selected owner costs, rent, total and occupied housing units, vacancy, and owner and renter tenure, plus a 2019 value comparison. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: ACS estimates cover multi-year survey periods, carry margins of error, and do not measure current listings, current financing terms, or a specific household's housing costs. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.

articles.title: New York 2026 county loan-limit landscape
articles.dek: How conforming and FHA one-unit limits vary across New York counties and how borrowers can use them responsibly.
articles.previewText: See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across New York without treating a limit as eligibility or pricing.
articles.metaDescription: New York 2026 county loan-limit landscape. See the range of 2026 conforming and FHA county limits across New York without treating a limit as eligibility or.
sourceLabels: U.S. Department of Housing and Urban Development
keyTakeaways: counties in the statewide limit summary: 62.

evidenceFacts.label: counties in the statewide limit summary

sections.heading: The conforming range across county records
body: New York's statewide summary covers 62 county or county-equivalent records. Within those records, the 2026 one-unit conforming limit runs from $832,750 to $1,209,750. The $832,750 to $1,209,750 range confirms that one conforming ceiling does not apply uniformly across New York. The 62 record count keeps the range tied to the statewide source coverage rather than an assumed national rule. The $832,750 to $1,209,750 amounts classify loan size within the published program table; they do not establish a borrower's capacity, a property's value, or the price of financing. Before using the $1,209,750 statewide maximum, county, legal unit count, and expected base loan amount must be known.

sections.heading: The FHA range follows its own county table
body: Across the same 62 New York records, the 2026 one-unit FHA limit spans $541,287 to $1,249,125. The $541,287 low and $1,249,125 high show that FHA's published boundary also varies within the state. The $541,287 floor and $1,249,125 high remain county-table values even when they match conforming amounts elsewhere. The FHA range should not be merged with the $832,750 to $1,209,750 conforming range, because the programs use different authorities and requirements. A $541,287 to $1,249,125 comparison can route loan-amount and product-category questions, but it cannot determine FHA eligibility, mortgage insurance, appraisal acceptability, occupancy compliance, cash needs, or approval.

sections.heading: Why county verification matters
body: 10 New York counties sit above the conforming baseline in this summary, while the full conforming range reaches from $832,750 to $1,209,750. That count shows where a statewide shortcut can fail: a property in a higher-limit county may use a different published ceiling than one at $832,750. The 10 figure does not mean those counties are more affordable, more desirable, or more likely to approve a loan. It only describes how many matched county rows exceed the baseline. The $1,209,750 maximum should therefore be treated as evidence that county lookup matters, not as the default New York limit. Before treating $1,209,750 as relevant, a borrower should identify the property's official county or county-equivalent area and then attach conventional, jumbo, or FHA labels to the desired loan amount.

sections.heading: Conforming and FHA ranges answer different questions
body: The highest one-unit conforming amount in New York is $1,209,750, while the highest FHA amount is $1,249,125; at the low end, the figures are $832,750 and $541,287. These pairs show program boundaries, not competing offers. A requested amount below $1,209,750 may still fall outside the applicable county's conforming limit, and an amount below $1,249,125 may still face FHA borrower and property requirements. Comparing $832,750, $1,209,750, $541,287, and $1,249,125 is useful only when county and unit count are held constant. The $1,209,750 and $1,249,125 ceilings leave rates, fees, mortgage insurance, appraisal, reserves, debt capacity, and product suitability as separate questions for the documented transaction.

sections.heading: A larger county limit is not greater affordability
body: A $1,209,750 conforming ceiling or $1,249,125 FHA ceiling does not make that loan amount affordable to a New York household, and the lower $832,750 or $541,287 figures do not describe home prices. Published $1,209,750 and $1,249,125 limits cannot establish borrower income, debts, credit, assets, occupancy, property condition, appraisal, insurance, reserves, rates, fees, or final approval. The $1,209,750 and $1,249,125 figures can also change with a new calendar year or corrected property geography. Keeping the 62 county-record scope and 2026 period attached to the figures prevents the statewide summary from being mistaken for a personalized product recommendation or a single amount that applies everywhere.

sections.heading: Use the range to reach the correct county row
body: The next step is to verify the selected property's New York county or county-equivalent area, legal unit count, price, down-payment plan, and expected base loan amount. Then compare that amount with the exact official row, using the $832,750 to $1,209,750 conforming range and $541,287 to $1,249,125 FHA range only as orientation. The 10 above-baseline count explains why this lookup matters, but it does not replace it. Keep the selected county row with the worksheet so the $1,209,750 and $1,249,125 statewide maximums cannot be mistaken for property-specific figures. After the 10 count prompts that county lookup, a licensed professional can explain current limits and borrower, property, mortgage-insurance, and documentation rules without presenting the largest statewide figure as available borrowing power.

articles.methodology: This article joins official 2026 FHFA conforming and HUD FHA county files by five-digit county FIPS, then summarizes the observed one-unit range and county counts for the state. Figures are reported as published for each cited geography and period. Derived percentages and differences use only the displayed source values; unavailable figures are omitted rather than estimated.
articles.limitations: State summaries can conceal special exceptions and multi-unit differences, and official program updates or property-location corrections can change the applicable row. The evidence is broad market context, not a property valuation, rate or payment quote, eligibility finding, underwriting decision, product recommendation, or prediction.
